title: Allan’s extra spending in Victoria is 10 times Albanese’s nationally
full_text:
The Allan government will pump 10 times as much extra money into Victoria over the next three years than its federal counterpart will commit across the entire national economy, underscoring concerns that high government spending is fuelling inflation.
A comparison of the “table of truths” in the Victorian and federal budgets reveals spending commitments in Victoria will cost the state’s operating bottom line $6 billion by 2028-29, while the equivalent figures for the Albanese government amounted to $600 million, analysis by independent economist Saul Eslake shows.
Victoria’s “policy variations” – spending – in 2026-27 will be $3.5 billion more than forecast in the midyear budget update handed down in December, $1.5 billion more in 2027-28 and $1.6 billion in 2028-29, totalling $6.6 billion.
In contrast, the net effect of policy decisions on the Albanese government’s operating balance – which does not include capital expenditure – in 2026-27 is tipped to be $3.8 billion more than what was forecast in December, before shrinking to $465 million in 2027-28. The Commonwealth is forecasting a net $4.2 billion saving in 2028-29.
“That’s roughly 10 times as much extra money into the Victorian economy than the feds have flagged into the entire national economy,” Eslake said. “That’s a big up yours to [RBA governor] Michele Bullock from Treasurer Jaclyn Symes,” Eslake said.
Long-time budget watcher Chris Richardson has described the reconciliation in federal Budget Paper No. 1 as the “table of truth”, which shows the net effect of a government’s spending and taxing decisions on the budget bottom line.
It sets out the starting point for the budget, the effect of those new policy decisions, the effect of parameter and other variations, and the impact on the budget.
Victoria’s version is in Budget Paper No. 2 and shows how the forward estimates for the next three years of the government’s preferred bottom line – the net operating balance – have changed since the midyear budget update.
The state budget papers revealed the “parameter variations” – developments over which the government has no direct influence – would have improved the net operating surplus over the three years to 2028-29 by $5 billion, mainly due to federal funding windfalls and goods and services tax receipts.
The net result from transactions was revised down by $900 million to $1 billion next financial year and by $500 million to $1.8 billion in 2027-28. It was revised up by $400 million to $1.9 billion in 2028-29.
While the figures in the “table of truth” are for the next three financial years, the budget papers also show the government has spent an extra $14 billion on “new output initiatives” – plus $4 billion on capital projects – in the five years to 2029-30.
“[The table] says the government really cannot control or contain its instinct to come up with new spending,” Eslake said.
After delivering the third consecutive interest rate rise this year, Bullock warned federal Treasurer Jim Chalmers and states that extra spending in their budgets would make it harder for the RBA to reduce inflation.
Inflation – which lifted from 3.7 per cent in February to 4.6 per cent in March on the back of higher fuel prices – is on track to rise past 5 per cent by June, according to federal Treasury modelling.
A Victorian government spokesman said on Thursday: “We won’t apologise for investing in frontline services and delivering cost-of-living relief for Victorians. We can do this because we’ve worked hard to deliver a budget surplus and we’re reducing debt as a share of the economy.”
Symes said on Wednesday: “Governments have an obligation to look after their citizens. We know that cost-of-living pressures is having a huge impact on Victorian families and that’s why the budget that I released last week confirmed that cost-of-living support.”
“We have de-inflationary measures and we have small measures that will have marginal impacts, minimal impacts, in relation to the claims that you make [about government spending fuelling inflation].”
The Victorian budget tipped a cash deficit of $7.7 billion for the general government sector in 2026-27, with net debt forecast to surge to $199.3 billion over the forward estimates to 24.4 per cent of the economy. Interest expenses in 2029-30 will balloon to $11.8 billion, or 9.56 per cent of revenue.
The state is forecast to achieve an operating surplus of $1 billion next financial year – almost half the $1.9 billion estimated in last year’s budget – after the government failed to find enough savings to offset billions of dollars in new spending.
All state and territory governments cite the net operating balance as the measure of their budget bottom line in contrast to the Commonwealth, which is focused on its cash balance.
The operating surplus accounts for recurrent expenses such as education and health, and includes federal funding for capital projects, but excludes $21.5 billion a year in infrastructure investment from the states. Victoria’s operating balance is an improvement on previous years, but the state cannot begin to pay down debt and reduce its interest expenses on its projected cumulative cash deficit of $30 billion over the four-year forward estimates.
Eslake said decisions made by the Hawke and Howard governments in the 1990s had made it harder to impose spending discipline on state and territory governments. The West Australian government, which is flush with mining royalties and the winner of a GST deal, also handed down a big spending budget last week.
Victorian Opposition Leader Jess Wilson said the Coalition would deliver a cash surplus by 2032 if she wins the November 28 state election.
“With $50 billion in cost blowouts and at least $15 billion lost to major project corruption, Labor’s gross mismanagement has long contributed to higher inflation and placed upward pressure on interest rates,” Wilson said.
“Victorians struggling to make ends meet face higher mortgage repayments, rents and everyday costs, all because Labor cannot manage money.”
The Victorian government was contacted for comment.
YXJ0aWNsZToyODk5NDFlZi0zYzQwLTRmNjUtYTgyNS1kMz
summary: The Victorian government plans to spend $6 billion more over the next three years compared to the federal government's $600 million, raising concerns about inflation. The extra spending in Victoria is seen as a challenge to the federal government's efforts to control rising prices.
author: Sumeyya Ilanbey
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Victoria's spending will total $6 billion by 2028-29.
Federal government's spending is only $600 million.
Inflation is expected to rise past 5% by June.
Victorian government defends spending as necessary for public services.
ticker_line: Victoria's spending outpaces federal budget tenfold
